text: Poorly controlled diabetes with hyperglycemia over the past couple of months
transcript_quote: The sugars have been running high — the doctor called it hyperglycemia — and I could feel it.

text: Polydipsia (excessive thirst)
transcript_quote: Thirsty all the time, dragging, foggy.

text: Fatigue and mental fogginess
transcript_quote: Thirsty all the time, dragging, foggy.

text: Intermittent blurred vision, worse in the afternoons
transcript_quote: my eyes started blurring, especially afternoons. Reading a pill bottle was like reading through wax paper.

text: Tingling/pins-and-needles in hands and feet, mostly at night, for a few months and worsening
transcript_quote: my hands and feet — pins and needles. At night mostly. Like they fell asleep and forgot to wake back up.

text: Denies falls
transcript_quote: No falls, knock wood.

text: Denies foot sores, burns, cuts, or non-healing wounds
transcript_quote: No sores. I check my feet — well, when I remember to.

text: Recent ophthalmology visit 2 weeks ago with dilated fundus exam using tropicamide
transcript_quote: Two weeks ago. They put in those stinging drops that blow your pupils up like an owl.

text: Continue metformin extended-release 500 mg once daily, unchanged, given with food at consistent time
transcript_quote: Just the metformin — the extended-release, 500. One a day.

text: Reports irregular meals and inconsistent medication adherence during recent stressful period
transcript_quote: meals got random. Crackers for dinner, then nothing until two the next afternoon. And when the meals go sideways, the pill schedule goes sideways with them.

text: Lives alone
transcript_quote: I live alone.

text: Financial hardship after dropping to part-time then stopping work
transcript_quote: Money got tight when I dropped to part-time, and tighter when I stopped.

text: Social isolation and grief following death of sister two years ago
transcript_quote: My sister passed two years ago, and she was my person — the phone does not ring much now.

text: Difficulty coping with self-management leading to disengagement from care
transcript_quote: I got so tired of managing it all — the sugars, the appointments, the everything — that I sort of stopped managing it.

text: History and physical examination performed
Initial patient assessment completed
Development of individualized plan of care
transcript_quote: Here is the plan — stop me anytime with questions.

text: Daily nursing care with pre-meal blood glucose checks and medication administration with meals

slots.order: nursing surveillance / blood glucose monitoring / med administration

transcript_quote: They will check your blood sugar before meals, give the metformin at the same time every day with actual food in front of you

text: Daily nursing foot checks as part of routine
transcript_quote: the nurses here will look at your feet every day, that is part of the routine

text: Dietitian consult for consistent carbohydrate meal planning and home-cookable diet education
transcript_quote: our dietitian will sit with you and build a version of it you can actually cook at home

text: Occupational therapy several times weekly for kitchen routines, medication organization, and energy pacing
transcript_quote: Occupational therapy will work with you several times a week — that is the practical side: kitchen routines, organizing the medications, pacing your energy

text: Physical therapy a couple of times for strength and balance
transcript_quote: Physical therapy will see you a couple of times for strength and balance, since you have been so run down.

text: Speech-language therapy for baseline swallowing check and cognitive strategies (memory aids, checklists)

slots.focus: swallowing baseline and cognitive routine support

transcript_quote: They will do a quick swallowing check as a baseline, but mostly they work on strategies for keeping complicated routines straight

text: Social work involvement for support and coping with isolation
transcript_quote: Our social worker is part of your team here — not because anything is wrong with you, but because rebuilding the routine goes better with company.

text: Pharmacist involvement as part of care team
transcript_quote: a whole crew of specialists who will be checking in on you regularly through the stay — dietitian, social services, the pharmacist

text: Continue/keep scheduled ophthalmology follow-up appointment
transcript_quote: You have a follow-up with them, and we will keep that on the calendar.

text: Pre-discharge assessment planned to evaluate ability to manage medications, meals, stairs, and home support needs
transcript_quote: Toward the end we do a full pre-discharge assessment: can you manage the medicines, the meals, the stairs, and what support you will need at home.

text: Anticipated length of stay a few weeks to achieve steady glucose control and improved strength
transcript_quote: a few weeks is the usual shape of it — long enough for steady sugars and steadier legs

text: Discharge from hospital procedure completed
Counseling that neuropathy and blurred vision symptoms often improve with steady glucose control but degree/timing not guaranteed
transcript_quote: they often improve as the sugars steady, but I cannot promise how much or how fast.

text: Physician will see patient on rounds the following morning; full nursing check-in this afternoon

slots.followup: physician rounds next morning, nursing intake this afternoon

transcript_quote: the nurses will do your full check-in this afternoon, and I will see you on rounds tomorrow morning

text: Counseling and emotional support regarding adjustment to admission and reframing stay as short-term rehabilitation
transcript_quote: this is a pit stop, not a parking spot. You are here to get the diabetes steadied and your strength back so that home works again.
